title: 2 Samuel 1
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 2 Samuel
paragraphs: 1 Saul died. After that, David came back from the battle against the Amalekites. He stayed in Ziklag for two days.
2 On the third day, a man arrived from Saul's camp. His clothes were torn and his head had dust on it. When he came to David, he fell to the ground and bowed before him.
3 "Where have you come from?" David asked. "I escaped from the camp of Israel," the man replied.
4 "What happened? Please tell me," David said. "The soldiers ran away from the battle," he replied. "Many of them fell and died. Saul and his son Jonathan are also dead."
5 Then David asked the young man, "How do you know Saul and his son Jonathan are dead?"
6 "I happened to be on Mount Gilboa," he replied. "Saul was there, leaning on his spear. The chariots and horsemen were coming close to him.
7 When he turned and saw me, he called to me. I answered, 'Here I am!'
8 'Who are you?' he asked. I told him, 'I am an Amalekite.'
9 Then he begged me, 'Stand over me and kill me. Great pain has taken hold of me, but I am still alive.'
10 So I stood over him and killed him. I knew he could not live after he had fallen. I took the crown from his head and the band from his arm. I have brought them here to you, my lord."
11 Then David took hold of his own clothes and tore them. All the men with him did the same.
12 They cried and did not eat until evening. They mourned for Saul, for his son Jonathan, for the people of the LORD, and for the house of Israel. They had all fallen by the sword.
13 David asked the young man who had brought the news, "Where are you from?" "I am the son of a foreigner," he answered. "I am an Amalekite."
14 So David asked him, "Why were you not afraid to lift your hand and kill the LORD's anointed king?"
15 Then David called one of the young men and said, "Go, kill him!" So the young man struck him down, and he died.
16 David said to the dead Amalekite, "Your blood is on your own head. Your own mouth has spoken against you. You said, 'I killed the LORD's anointed king.'"
17 Then David sang this sad song for Saul and his son Jonathan.
18 He ordered that the people of Judah be taught the Song of the Bow. It is written in the Book of Jashar:
19 "Your glory, O Israel, lies dead on your hills. How the mighty have fallen!
20 Do not tell it in Gath. Do not say it in the streets of Ashkelon. The daughters of the Philistines must not be glad. The daughters of those without faith must not rejoice.
21 O mountains of Gilboa, let no dew or rain fall on you. Let no fields give grain for offerings. For there the shield of the strong was thrown down. The shield of Saul is no longer rubbed with oil.
22 From the blood of the dead, from the bodies of strong warriors, the bow of Jonathan did not turn back. The sword of Saul did not return empty.
23 Saul and Jonathan were loved and dear in life. In death they were not parted. They were faster than eagles. They were stronger than lions.
24 O daughters of Israel, cry for Saul. He dressed you in red and fine clothes. He put gold jewels on your clothes.
25 How the mighty have fallen in the middle of battle! Jonathan lies dead on your hills.
26 Jonathan, my brother, your love to me was wonderful, surpassing the love of women. I am very sad for you.
27 How the mighty have fallen! The weapons of war are destroyed!"

title: 2 Samuel 2
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 2 Samuel
paragraphs: 1 Some time later, David asked the LORD, "Should I go up to one of the towns of Judah?" The LORD answered, "Go up." Then David asked, "Where should I go?" The LORD replied, "To Hebron."
2 So David went there with his two wives, Ahinoam of Jezreel and Abigail the widow of Nabal of Carmel.
3 David also took the men who were with him, each with his family. They settled in the towns near Hebron.
4 Then the men of Judah came to Hebron. There they poured oil on David to make him king over the house of Judah. They told David, "The men of Jabesh-gilead buried Saul."
5 So David sent messengers to the men of Jabesh-gilead. He said, "May the LORD bless you. You showed this kindness to Saul your lord when you buried him.
6 Now may the LORD show you steady love and faithfulness. I will also show you the same kindness, because you have done this.
7 Now then, be strong and brave. Saul your lord is dead. But the house of Judah has poured oil on me to make me their king."
8 Meanwhile, Abner son of Ner was the leader of Saul's army. He took Saul's son Ish-bosheth and moved him to Mahanaim.
9 He made him king over Gilead, Asher, Jezreel, Ephraim, and Benjamin — over all Israel.
10 Saul's son Ish-bosheth was forty years old when he started to rule as king over Israel. He ruled for two years. But the house of Judah followed David.
11 David was king in Hebron over the house of Judah for seven years and six months.
12 One day Abner son of Ner and the men of Ish-bosheth son of Saul marched from Mahanaim to Gibeon.
13 So Joab son of Zeruiah and the men of David marched out too. They met them by the pool of Gibeon. The two camps took places on opposite sides of the pool.
14 Then Abner said to Joab, "Let the young men get up and fight in front of us." Joab replied, "Let them get up."
15 So they got up and were counted — twelve for Benjamin and Ish-bosheth son of Saul, and twelve for David.
16 Then each man grabbed his enemy by the head. He pushed his sword into his enemy's side. They all fell together. So people call this place in Gibeon "Helkath-hazzurim."
17 The battle that day was hard. The men of David defeated Abner and the men of Israel.
18 The three sons of Zeruiah were there: Joab, Abishai, and Asahel. Asahel could run fast like a wild deer.
19 Asahel chased Abner. He did not turn to the right or the left as he chased him.
20 Abner looked back and said, "Is that you, Asahel?" Asahel replied, "It is."
21 So Abner told him, "Turn to your right or your left. Grab one of the young men and take his weapons for yourself." But Asahel would not stop chasing him.
22 Again Abner warned Asahel, "Stop chasing me. Why should I strike you to the ground? How could I face your brother Joab?"

23 But Asahel refused to turn away. So Abner pushed the back end of his spear into his stomach. It came out his back. He fell dead on the spot. Every man stopped when he came to the place where Asahel had fallen and died.
24 But Joab and Abishai chased Abner. By sunset, they had gone as far as the hill of Ammah, opposite Giah, on the way to the wilderness of Gibeon.
25 The Benjamites came to Abner. They formed one group and stood together on top of a hill.
26 Then Abner called out to Joab, "Must the fighting kill people forever? Do you not see that this will only end in sadness? How long before you tell the troops to stop chasing their brothers?"
27 Joab replied, "As sure as God lives, if you had not spoken up, the troops would have chased their brothers until morning."
28 So Joab blew the horn. All the troops stopped. They no longer chased Israel or kept fighting.
29 That whole night Abner and his men marched through the Arabah. They crossed the Jordan. They marched all morning and arrived at Mahanaim.
30 When Joab came back from chasing Abner, he gathered all the troops. Besides Asahel, nineteen of David's men were missing.
31 But they had killed 360 Benjamites who were with Abner.
32 Later, they took Asahel and buried him in his father's tomb in Bethlehem. Then Joab and his men marched all night. They reached Hebron as the sun came up.

title: 2 Samuel 3
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 2 Samuel
paragraphs: 1 The war between Saul's family and David's family lasted a long time. David grew stronger and stronger. Saul's family grew weaker and weaker.
2 Sons were born to David in Hebron. His first son was Amnon. His mother was Ahinoam of Jezreel.
3 His second son was Chileab. His mother was Abigail, the widow of Nabal of Carmel. His third son was Absalom. His mother was Maacah, daughter of King Talmai of Geshur.
4 His fourth son was Adonijah. His mother was Haggith. His fifth son was Shephatiah. His mother was Abital.
5 His sixth son was Ithream. His mother was Eglah, David's wife. These sons were born to David in Hebron.
6 The war went on between Saul's family and David's family. During this time, Abner made his place stronger in Saul's family.
7 Saul had a second wife named Rizpah, the daughter of Aiah. Ish-bosheth asked Abner, "Why did you sleep with my father's wife?"

8 Abner became very angry at this. He said, "Am I the head of a dog from Judah? All this time I have been loyal to your father Saul's family. I have been loyal to his brothers and his friends. I did not give you to David. Now you say I did wrong with this woman!
9 May God punish me badly! The LORD promised David something. I must do it for David.
10 The LORD will take the kingdom from Saul's family. He will set up David's throne over Israel and Judah, from Dan to Beersheba."
11 Ish-bosheth was afraid of Abner. He did not dare to say another word to him.
12 Then Abner sent messengers to David. They said, "Whose land is this? Make an agreement with me. My hand will help you bring all Israel to you."
13 David replied, "Good. I will make an agreement with you. But I want one thing. Do not come to me unless you bring Saul's daughter Michal with you."
14 Then David sent messengers to Ish-bosheth, Saul's son. He said, "Give me back my wife Michal. I promised to marry her for one hundred Philistine skins."
15 So Ish-bosheth sent and took Michal from her husband Paltiel, son of Laish.
16 Her husband followed her. He cried all the way to Bahurim. Then Abner said to him, "Go back." So he returned home.
17 Abner talked with the elders of Israel. He said, "In the past you wanted David as your king.
18 Now act. The LORD told David this: 'Through My servant David I will save My people Israel. I will save them from the Philistines and all their enemies.'"
19 Abner also spoke to the people of Benjamin. He went to Hebron to tell David what Israel and all of Benjamin wanted.
20 Abner and twenty of his men came to David at Hebron. David made a big meal for them.

21 Then Abner said to David, "Let me go now. I will gather all Israel to my lord the king. They will make an agreement with you. You will rule over all that you want." So David sent Abner away. He went in peace.
22 Just then David's soldiers and Joab returned from a raid. They brought many goods with them. But Abner was not with David in Hebron. David had already sent him away in peace.
23 Joab and all his troops arrived. Someone told him, "Abner, son of Ner, came to see the king. The king sent him away in peace."
24 So Joab went to the king. He said, "What have you done? Look, Abner came to you. Why did you let him go? Now he is getting away!
25 Surely you know that Abner, son of Ner, came to trick you. He came to watch what you are doing."
26 Then Joab left David. He sent messengers after Abner. They brought him back from the well of Sirah. But David did not know about it.

27 When Abner returned to Hebron, Joab pulled him aside into the gate. Joab acted as if he wanted to speak in private. There Joab pushed a knife into his stomach. So Abner died because of the blood of Joab's brother Asahel.
28 Later, David heard about this. He said, "I and my kingdom are forever not guilty before the LORD. We are not guilty of the blood of Abner, son of Ner.

29 May it fall on the heads of Joab and all his father's family. May Joab's family always have someone with sickness or skin disease. May they have someone who walks with a stick. May they have someone who dies by the sword or has no food."
30 (Joab and his brother Abishai killed Abner. Abner had killed their brother Asahel in the battle at Gibeon.)
31 Then David ordered Joab and all the people with him. He said, "Tear your clothes. Put on rough cloth for sadness. Show sadness for Abner." King David himself walked behind Abner's funeral bed.
32 They buried Abner in Hebron. The king cried loudly at Abner's tomb. All the people cried.
33 The king sang this sad song for Abner. He sang, "Should Abner die the death of a fool?
34 Your hands were not tied. Your feet were not tied with chains. You fell like a man who falls before wicked people." All the people cried again.

35 Then all the people came to David. They asked him to eat something while it was still day. But David made a promise. He said, "May God punish me badly! I will not taste bread or anything else before the sun sets!"
36 All the people saw this and were happy. In fact, everything the king did made them happy.
37 So on that day all the troops and all Israel knew the truth. The king had no part in killing Abner, son of Ner.
38 Then the king said to his servants, "Do you not see? A great leader has fallen today in Israel.
39 I am weak today, though I was chosen as king by oil. These men, the sons of Zeruiah, are too strong for me. May the LORD pay back the one who does evil for his evil!"

title: 2 Samuel 4
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 2 Samuel
paragraphs: 1 Ish-bosheth was Saul's son. He heard that Abner had died in Hebron. He lost his strength, and all Israel was afraid.

2 Saul's son had two men. They were leaders of groups that attacked others. One was named Baanah. The other was named Rechab. They were sons of Rimmon the Beerothite, from the tribe of Benjamin. Beeroth is counted as part of Benjamin,
3 because the Beerothites ran away to Gittaim. They have lived there as strangers to this day.

4 Jonathan son of Saul had a son who could not walk well. He was five years old when the news about Saul and Jonathan came from Jezreel. The woman who cared for him picked him up and ran. But she hurried to escape, and he fell. He could not walk after that. His name was Mephibosheth.
5 Rechab and Baanah, the sons of Rimmon the Beerothite, set out. They arrived at the house of Ish-bosheth in the heat of the day. The king was taking his afternoon sleep.
6 They went inside the house as if to get some wheat. They pushed a knife into his stomach. Then Rechab and his brother Baanah ran away quietly.
7 They had gone into the house while Ish-bosheth was lying on his bed. They killed him with a knife. Then they cut off his head, took it, and traveled all night through the Arabah valley.

8 They brought the head of Ish-bosheth to David at Hebron. They said to the king, "Here is the head of Ish-bosheth son of Saul. He was your enemy who wanted to kill you. Today the LORD has paid back my lord the king against Saul and his family."
9 But David answered Rechab and his brother Baanah, the sons of Rimmon the Beerothite. He said, "The LORD is alive, and I promise this. He has saved my life from all trouble.
10 Once a man told me, 'Look, Saul is dead.' He thought he brought good news. But I grabbed him and killed him at Ziklag. That was his reward for his news!
11 Now wicked men have killed a good man in his own house and on his own bed. I must punish you for his death. I will take you away from this earth!"

12 So David gave an order to his young men. They killed Rechab and Baanah. They cut off their hands and feet. They hung their bodies by the pool in Hebron. But they took the head of Ish-bosheth and buried it in Abner's grave in Hebron.

title: 2 Samuel 5
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 2 Samuel
paragraphs: 1 Then all the tribes of Israel came to David at Hebron. They said, "Look, we are your own family.
2 Even before, when Saul was king, you led Israel out and brought them back. The LORD said to you, 'You will care for My people Israel. You will be their ruler.'"
3 So all the leaders of Israel came to the king at Hebron. King David made a promise with them before the LORD. They poured oil on him to make him king over Israel.
4 David was thirty years old when he became king. He ruled for forty years.
5 In Hebron he ruled over Judah seven years and six months. In Jerusalem he ruled over all Israel and Judah for thirty-three years.

6 The king and his men marched to Jerusalem to fight the Jebusites who lived there. The Jebusites said to David, "You will never get in here. Even blind and lame people can push you back." They thought, "David cannot get in here."
7 But David captured the strong city of Zion. That is the City of David.

8 On that day David said, "Whoever attacks the Jebusites must use the water tunnel. They will reach the lame and blind people whom David hates." That is why people say, "The blind and the lame will never enter the palace."
9 So David lived in the strong city. He called it the City of David. He built it up all around, from the stone walls inward.
10 David became greater and greater. The LORD God of Hosts was with him.
11 Hiram king of Tyre sent messengers to David. He also sent cedar wood, wood workers, and stone workers. They built a palace for David.
12 Then David knew that the LORD had made him king over Israel. The LORD had lifted up his kingdom high for the good of His people Israel.
13 After David came from Hebron, he took more second wives and wives in Jerusalem. More sons and daughters were born to him.
14 These are the names of his children born in Jerusalem: Shammua, Shobab, Nathan, Solomon,
15 Ibhar, Elishua, Nepheg, Japhia,
16 Elishama, Eliada, and Eliphelet.
17 The Philistines heard that David had been made king over Israel. So they all came to look for him. But David heard about it and went down to the safe place.
18 The Philistines had come and spread out in the Valley of Rephaim.
19 So David asked the LORD, "Should I go up against the Philistines? Will You hand them over to me?" The LORD answered, "Go. I will surely hand the Philistines over to you."
20 So David went to Baal-perazim. There he defeated the Philistines. He said, "Like a flood that breaks through, the LORD has burst out against my enemies." So he called that place Baal-perazim.
21 The Philistines left their idols there. David and his men carried them away.
22 Once again the Philistines came up and spread out in the Valley of Rephaim.
23 So David asked the LORD. The LORD answered, "Do not march straight up. Go around behind them. Attack them in front of the balsam trees.
24 When you hear the sound of marching in the tops of the balsam trees, move quickly. This will mean that the LORD has gone out before you to strike the Philistine camp."
25 So David did what the LORD had told him. He defeated the Philistines all the way from Gibeon to Gezer.

title: 2 Samuel 6
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 2 Samuel
paragraphs: 1 David again gathered the chosen soldiers of Israel — thirty thousand in all.
2 He and all his troops left for Baale of Judah. They went to bring the ark of God from there. The ark is called by the name of the LORD of Hosts. He sits on his throne above the cherubim on the ark.
3 They put the ark of God on a new cart. They brought it from the house of Abinadab on the hill. Uzzah and Ahio, the sons of Abinadab, were leading the new cart.
4 They brought the ark of God with them. Ahio was walking in front of the ark.
5 David and all the house of Israel were celebrating before the LORD. They played many music instruments — harps, drums, and cymbals.
6 When they came to the place for grain at Nacon, the oxen tripped. Uzzah reached out and grabbed the ark of God.
7 The LORD was angry with Uzzah. God killed him right there for his lack of respect. He died beside the ark of God.
8 Then David became angry because the LORD had struck out at Uzzah. So he named that place Perez-uzzah. It is still called this today.
9 That day David was afraid of the LORD. He asked, "How can the ark of the LORD ever come to me?"
10 So David did not want to move the ark to the City of David. Instead, he took it to the house of Obed-edom from Gath.
11 The ark of the LORD stayed in the house of Obed-edom for three months. And the LORD blessed him and all his family.

12 Someone told King David, "The LORD has blessed Obed-edom and all he owns. This is because of the ark of God." So David went to the house of Obed-edom. He brought the ark up to the City of David with joy.
13 The men carried the ark of the LORD. When they had walked six steps, David offered a sacrifice. He gave an ox and a young fat cow.
14 David wore a linen ephod. He danced with all his strength before the LORD.
15 He and all the house of Israel brought up the ark of the LORD. They came with shouts and the sound of the horn.
16 The ark of the LORD was entering the City of David. Saul's daughter Michal looked down from a window. She saw King David leaping and dancing before the LORD. And she hated him in her heart.
17 They brought the ark of the LORD and set it in its place. It went inside the tent that David had set up for it. Then David offered burnt offerings and peace offerings before the LORD.
18 When David had finished the burnt offerings and peace offerings, he blessed the people. He blessed them in the name of the LORD of Hosts.
19 Then he gave food to every man and woman in the crowd of Israel. Each one got bread, a date cake, and a raisin cake. And all the people went home.

20 David returned home to bless his own family. Saul's daughter Michal came out to meet him. She said, "How the king of Israel acted so grandly today! You showed your body to your servants' young women today. You acted like a shameful man."
21 But David said to Michal, "I was dancing before the LORD. He chose me over your father and all his house. He made me ruler over the LORD's people Israel. I will celebrate before the LORD.
22 I will make myself even lower and more humble than this. Yet those young women you spoke of will respect me."
23 And Michal, the daughter of Saul, had no children until the day she died.

title: 2 Samuel 7
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 2 Samuel
paragraphs: 1 King David now lived in his palace. The LORD had given him rest from all his enemies around him.
2 He said to Nathan the prophet, "Look, I live in a house of cedar. But the ark of God still stays in a tent."
3 Nathan replied to the king, "Go and do all that is in your heart. The LORD is with you."
4 But that night the word of the LORD came to Nathan. He said,
5 "Go and tell my servant David that this is what the LORD says: Will you build me a house to live in?
6 I have not lived in a house from the day I brought Israel up out of Egypt. I have moved about with a tent as my home.
7 I traveled with all the people of Israel. Did I ever ask any leader I chose to care for my people, 'Why have you not built me a house of cedar?'
8 Now tell my servant David that this is what the LORD of Hosts says: I took you from the fields. You followed the sheep. Now you rule my people Israel.
9 I have been with you wherever you have gone. I have cut off all your enemies before you. Now I will make your name as great as the greatest in the land.
10 I will give my people Israel a place of their own. I will plant them there, and they will be safe. Evil people will not hurt them anymore, as they did before,
11 from the day I gave judges to my people Israel. I will give you rest from all your enemies. The LORD himself will establish a house for you.
12 When your days are over and you rest with your fathers, I will raise up your offspring to succeed you, who will come from your own body, and I will establish his kingdom.
13 He is the one who will build a house for my Name, and I will establish the throne of his kingdom forever.
14 I will be his father, and he will be my son. When he does wrong, I will correct him with the rod of men. I will use the blows of human hands.
15 But my faithful love will never leave him, as I took it from Saul. I moved Saul out of your way.
16 Your house and your kingdom will endure forever before me; your throne will be established forever."
17 So Nathan told David all the words of this whole vision.
18 Then King David went in and sat before the LORD. He said, "Who am I, O Lord GOD? What is my family, that you have brought me this far?
19 And this was still a small thing in your eyes, O Lord GOD. You have also spoken about the future of your servant's house. Is this your way with man, O Lord GOD?
20 What more can David say to you? For you know your servant, O Lord GOD.
21 For the sake of your word and your own heart, you have done this great thing. You have shown it to your servant.
22 How great you are, O Lord GOD! There is no one like you. There is no God but you. We have heard this with our own ears.

23 And who is like your people Israel? They are the one nation on earth whom God went out to rescue. You made them a people for yourself and made a name for yourself. You did great and mighty wonders. You drove out nations and their gods before your people. You rescued them for yourself from Egypt.
24 You have made your people Israel your very own forever. You, O LORD, have become their God.
25 And now, O LORD God, keep forever the word you have spoken about your servant and his house. Do as you have promised.
26 Then your name will be great forever. People will say, 'The LORD of Hosts is God over Israel.' And the house of your servant David will stand firm before you.
27 For you, O LORD of Hosts, the God of Israel, have shown this to your servant. You said, 'I will build a house for you.' So your servant has found courage to pray this prayer to you.
28 And now, O Lord GOD, you are God! Your words are true. You have promised this good thing to your servant.
29 Now please bless the house of your servant. May it continue forever before you. For you, O Lord GOD, have spoken. With your blessing the house of your servant will be blessed forever."

title: 2 Samuel 8
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 2 Samuel
paragraphs: 1 Some time later, David defeated the Philistines. He took control of them. He took Metheg-ammah from their hand.

2 David also defeated the Moabites. He made them lie down on the ground. Then he measured them off with a cord. With two lengths he marked those to die. With one length he marked those to live. So the Moabites came under David's rule and paid him tax.
3 David also defeated Hadadezer son of Rehob, king of Zobah. Hadadezer had marched out to win back his power along the Euphrates River.

4 David captured a thousand chariots and seven thousand chariot drivers from him. He also captured twenty thousand soldiers on foot. He cut the leg muscles of all the horses. But he kept a hundred for the chariots.
5 The Arameans of Damascus came to help King Hadadezer of Zobah. So David killed twenty-two thousand of their men.
6 Then David put army posts in Aram of Damascus. The Arameans came under David's rule and paid him tax. So the LORD gave David victory everywhere he went.
7 David took the gold shields of Hadadezer's officers. He brought them to Jerusalem.
8 From Betah and Berothai, cities of Hadadezer, King David took a large amount of bronze.
9 King Toi of Hamath heard that David had defeated all of Hadadezer's army.
10 So Toi sent his son Joram to greet King David. Joram blessed David for fighting and defeating Hadadezer. Hadadezer had been at war with Toi. Joram brought items made of silver, gold, and bronze.
11 King David set these apart for the LORD. He also set apart the silver and gold from all the nations he had defeated —
12 from Edom and Moab, from the Ammonites, Philistines, and Amalekites. He also set apart the things taken in war from Hadadezer son of Rehob, king of Zobah.
13 David became famous when he returned. He had killed eighteen thousand Edomites in the Valley of Salt.
14 He put army posts all through Edom. All the Edomites came under David's rule. So the LORD gave David victory everywhere he went.
15 David ruled all Israel as king. He ruled fairly and rightly for all his people.
16 Joab son of Zeruiah was in charge of the army. Jehoshaphat son of Ahilud was the records officer.
17 Zadok son of Ahitub and Ahimelech son of Abiathar were priests. Seraiah was the writer for the king.
18 Benaiah son of Jehoiada was in charge of the Cherethites and Pelethites. And David's sons were priestly leaders.

title: 2 Samuel 9
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 2 Samuel
paragraphs: 1 Then David asked, "Is anyone left from Saul's family? I want to be kind to them because of Jonathan."
2 Saul's family had a servant named Ziba. They called him to David. The king asked, "Are you Ziba?" "I am your servant," he replied.
3 So the king asked, "Is anyone left of Saul's family? I want to show them God's own kindness." Ziba answered, "Jonathan still has a son. He cannot walk on both feet."
4 "Where is he?" the king asked. Ziba said, "He is in Lo-debar, at the house of Machir son of Ammiel."
5 So King David sent for him. They brought him from the house of Machir son of Ammiel in Lo-debar.
6 Mephibosheth was the son of Jonathan, the son of Saul. He came to David and bowed his face to the ground. David said, "Mephibosheth!" "I am your servant," he replied.
7 "Do not be afraid," David said. "I will be kind to you because of your father Jonathan. I will give back to you all the land of your grandfather Saul. And you will always eat at my table."
8 Mephibosheth bowed low and said, "Who am I, your servant? Why do you care about a dead dog like me?"
9 Then the king called Saul's servant Ziba. He said, "I have given your master's grandson everything that belonged to Saul and his family.

10 You, your sons, and your servants must work the land for him. Bring in the crops so your master's grandson has food to eat. But Mephibosheth, your master's grandson, will always eat at my table." Now Ziba had fifteen sons and twenty servants.
11 Ziba said to the king, "Your servant will do everything the king, my lord, has told me." So Mephibosheth ate at David's table like one of the king's own sons.
12 Mephibosheth had a young son named Mica. All who lived in Ziba's house were servants of Mephibosheth.
13 So Mephibosheth lived in Jerusalem. He always ate at the king's table. And he could not walk on both feet.

title: 2 Samuel 10
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 2 Samuel
paragraphs: 1 Some time later, the king of the Ammonites died. His son Hanun became king after him.

2 David said, "I will show kindness to Hanun son of Nahash. His father showed kindness to me." So David sent some servants to comfort Hanun about his father. But when they arrived in the land of the Ammonites,

3 the leaders of the Ammonites said to Hanun their lord, "David has sent you men to comfort you. But do you really think he wants to honor your father? Has David not sent his servants to look at the city, spy on it, and take it down?"
4 So Hanun took David's servants. He shaved off half of each man's beard. He cut off their clothes at the hips. Then he sent them away.
5 When David heard about this, he sent messengers to meet the men. They had been deeply shamed. The king told them, "Stay in Jericho until your beards grow back. Then return."

6 The Ammonites saw that they had made David very angry. So they hired twenty thousand Aramean soldiers on foot from Beth-rehob and Zobah. They also hired a thousand men from the king of Maacah and twelve thousand men from Tob.
7 When David heard this, he sent Joab and the whole army of strong men.
8 The Ammonites marched out and lined up for battle at the city gate. The Arameans of Zobah and Rehob and the men of Tob and Maacah stood alone out in the open field.
9 Joab saw the battle lines in front of him and behind him. So he chose some of the best soldiers of Israel. He lined them up against the Arameans.
10 He put the rest of the troops under his brother Abishai. Abishai lined them up against the Ammonites.
11 Joab said, "If the Arameans are too strong for me, you will come and help me. If the Ammonites are too strong for you, I will come and help you.
12 Be strong. Let us fight bravely for our people and for the cities of our God. May the LORD do what is good in His eyes."
13 So Joab and his troops moved forward to fight the Arameans. The Arameans ran away from him.
14 When the Ammonites saw that the Arameans had run away, they also ran away from Abishai. They went into the city. So Joab stopped fighting the Ammonites and came back to Jerusalem.
15 The Arameans saw that Israel had defeated them. So they gathered together again.
16 Hadadezer sent messengers to bring more Arameans from across the Euphrates River. They came to Helam. Shobach, the leader of Hadadezer's army, led them.
17 When David heard about this, he gathered all Israel. He crossed the Jordan and went to Helam. The Arameans lined up against David and fought him.
18 But the Arameans ran away from Israel. David killed seven hundred chariot drivers and forty thousand soldiers on foot. He also killed Shobach the leader of their army. Shobach died there.
19 All the kings under Hadadezer's rule saw that Israel had defeated them. So they made peace with Israel and came under their rule. The Arameans were afraid to help the Ammonites anymore.

title: 2 Samuel 11
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 2 Samuel
paragraphs: 1 In the spring, when kings go out to war, David sent Joab with all the army of Israel. They destroyed the Ammonites and attacked the city of Rabbah. But David stayed in Jerusalem.
2 One evening David got up from his bed. He walked on the roof of the palace. From the roof he saw a woman washing herself. She was very beautiful.
3 So David sent someone to ask about the woman. They told him, "This is Bathsheba, the daughter of Eliam. She is the wife of Uriah the Hittite."
4 Then David sent messengers to bring her to him. She came, and he slept with her. (She had just finished her time of being unclean.) Then she went home.
5 The woman became pregnant. She sent word to David and said, "I am pregnant."
6 So David sent orders to Joab. He said, "Send me Uriah the Hittite." So Joab sent Uriah to David.
7 When Uriah came, David asked how Joab and the soldiers were doing in the war.
8 Then David said to Uriah, "Go down to your house and wash your feet." So Uriah left the palace. A gift from the king was sent after him.
9 But Uriah slept at the door of the palace with all his master's servants. He did not go down to his house.
10 Someone told David, "Uriah did not go home." So David asked Uriah, "Haven't you just come from a journey? Why didn't you go home?"

11 Uriah answered, "The ark and Israel and Judah are living in tents. My master Joab and his soldiers are camped in the open field. How can I go to my house to eat and drink and sleep with my wife? As you yourself live, I will not do such a thing!"
12 David said to Uriah, "Stay here one more day. Tomorrow I will send you back." So Uriah stayed in Jerusalem that day and the next.
13 Then David asked Uriah to eat and drink with him. He made Uriah drunk. In the evening Uriah went out to sleep on his bed with his master's servants. But he did not go home.
14 The next morning David wrote a letter to Joab. He sent it with Uriah.
15 In the letter David wrote, "Put Uriah at the front of the hardest fighting. Then pull back and leave him, so he will be hit and killed."
16 So Joab attacked the city. He put Uriah at the place where he saw the strongest enemy soldiers.
17 The men of the city came out and fought against Joab. Some of David's servants fell. Uriah the Hittite also died.
18 Joab sent a full report of the battle to David.
19 He told the messenger, "When you have given the king all the news of the battle,
20 the king may become very angry. He may ask you, 'Why did you go so close to the city to fight? Did you not know they would shoot from the top of the wall?

21 Who killed Abimelech son of Jerubbesheth? Was it not a woman who dropped a heavy stone on him from the wall? He died in Thebez. Why did you go so close to the wall?' If he asks this, then say, 'Your servant Uriah the Hittite is also dead.'"
22 So the messenger went and told David everything Joab had sent him to say.
23 The messenger said to David, "The men were stronger than us and came out against us in the field. But we pushed them back to the gate.
24 Then the archers shot at your servants from the wall. Some of the king's servants were killed. And your servant Uriah the Hittite is also dead."

25 Then David told the messenger, "Say this to Joab: 'Do not let this matter trouble you. The sword kills one man as it kills another. Make your attack on the city stronger and tear it down.' Encourage Joab with these words."
26 When Uriah's wife heard that her husband was dead, she cried for him.
27 When the time of crying was over, David brought her to his house. She became his wife and gave him a son. But the thing David had done displeased the LORD.

title: 2 Samuel 12
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 2 Samuel
paragraphs: 1 Then the LORD sent Nathan to David. Nathan came and said, "Two men lived in a city. One was rich, and the other was poor.
2 The rich man had many sheep and cattle.

3 But the poor man had only one little female lamb. He had bought her. He raised her, and she grew up with him and his children. She shared his food. She drank from his cup. She slept in his arms. She was like a daughter to him.
4 One day a traveler came to the rich man. The rich man did not want to take his own sheep or cattle. So he took the poor man's lamb. He cooked it for his guest."
5 David became very angry at the man. He said to Nathan, "As the LORD lives, the man who did this should die!
6 He had no pity. So he must pay back four times for the lamb."
7 Then Nathan said to David, "You are the man! This is what the LORD, the God of Israel, says: 'I made you king over Israel. I saved you from Saul.
8 I gave you your master's house and his wives. I gave you the house of Israel and Judah. If that was not enough, I would have given you more.
9 So why did you turn away from the LORD's word? You did evil in his sight. You killed Uriah the Hittite with a sword. You took his wife as your own. The Ammonites' sword killed him.
10 Now a sword will always be in your house. You hated me and took Uriah the Hittite's wife.'
11 This is what the LORD says: 'I will bring trouble against you from your own house. Before your eyes I will take your wives. I will give them to another man. He will lie with them in full daylight.
12 You did this in secret. But I will do this thing in full daylight before all Israel.'"
13 Then David said to Nathan, "I have sinned against the LORD." Nathan said, "The LORD has taken away your sin. You are not going to die.
14 But you have shown great scorn for the LORD's word. So the son born to you will surely die."
15 Then Nathan went home. The LORD made the child sick. Uriah's wife had given birth to this child for David.
16 David begged God for the boy. He did not eat. He went into his house and lay on the ground in rough cloth all night.
17 The older men in his house stood beside him. They tried to help him up from the ground. But he would not get up. He would not eat with them.

18 On the seventh day the child died. But David's servants were afraid to tell him. They said, "Look, while the child was alive, we spoke to him. But he would not listen. So how can we tell him the child is dead? He may harm himself."
19 David saw that his servants were whispering. He understood that the child was dead. So he asked them, "Is the child dead?" "He is dead," they answered.
20 Then David got up from the ground. He washed and put oil on himself. He changed his clothes. He went into the house of the LORD and worshiped. Then he went home. He asked for food, and he ate.
21 His servants asked him, "What is this you have done? While the child was alive, you did not eat and you wept. But when he died, you got up and ate."
22 David answered, "While the child was alive, I did not eat and I wept. I thought, 'Who knows? The LORD may be kind to me and let him live.'
23 But now he is dead. Why should I not eat? Can I bring him back? I will go to him, but he will not come back to me."
24 Then David comforted his wife Bathsheba. He went to her and lay with her. She gave birth to a son. They named him Solomon. Now the LORD loved the child.
25 The LORD sent word through Nathan the prophet. He told them to name the child Jedidiah, which means "loved by the LORD."
26 Meanwhile, Joab fought against Rabbah of the Ammonites. He captured the royal city.
27 Then Joab sent messengers to David. He said, "I have fought against Rabbah. I have captured the city's water source.
28 Now bring the rest of the troops. Attack the city and take it. If not, I will take the city. Then it will be named after me."
29 So David brought together all the troops. He went to Rabbah. He fought against it and captured it.
30 Then he took the crown from their king's head. It was a great weight of gold and had precious stones in it. They placed it on David's head. David also took many goods from the city.

31 David brought out the people there. He put them to work with saws, iron picks, and axes. He made them work at the brick ovens. He did the same to all the Ammonite cities. Then David and all his troops returned to Jerusalem.

title: 2 Samuel 13
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 2 Samuel
paragraphs: 1 Some time later, Amnon, David's son, fell in love with Tamar. She was the beautiful sister of Absalom, David's son.
2 Amnon was sick with worry over his sister Tamar. She had no husband, and he could not see how to reach her.
3 Amnon had a friend named Jonadab. He was the son of David's brother Shimeah. Jonadab was a very clever man.
4 Jonadab asked Amnon, "Why are you, the king's son, so sad every morning? Won't you tell me?" Amnon said, "I am in love with Tamar, my brother Absalom's sister."
5 Jonadab told him, "Lie on your bed and pretend to be sick. When your father comes, ask him this: 5b 'Please let my sister Tamar come and give me food. Let her make it where I can see and feed me.'"
6 So Amnon lay down and pretended to be sick. When the king came, Amnon said, "Please let my sister Tamar come. 6b Let her make two cakes where I can see, so I can eat from her hand."
7 Then David sent a message to Tamar at the palace. He said, "Go to your brother Amnon's house and make food for him."
8 So Tamar went to her brother Amnon's house. He was lying down. She took dough, shaped it, and made cakes where he could see.
9 Then she brought the pan and set it down before him. But he refused to eat. "Send everyone away!" Amnon said. So everyone went out.
10 Then Amnon said to Tamar, "Bring the food into the bedroom, so I can eat from your hand." Tamar took the cakes and went to her brother Amnon's bedroom.
11 When she brought them to him to eat, he grabbed her. He said, "Come to bed with me, my sister!"
12 "No, my brother!" she cried. "Do not shame me. Such a thing is never done in Israel. Do not do this shameful thing!
13 Where could I take my shame? And you would be like a fool in Israel! Please speak to the king. He will not keep me from you."
14 But Amnon refused to listen to her. He was stronger, so he forced her and slept with her.
15 Then Amnon hated Tamar very much. His hatred was greater than the love he had before. "Get up!" he said. "Go away!"
16 "No," she said. "Sending me away is worse than the wrong you have already done to me!" But he refused to listen.
17 Instead, he called his servant and said, "Throw this woman out and lock the door behind her!"
18 So Amnon's servant threw her out and locked the door. Tamar was wearing a long colorful robe. The king's daughters who had no husband wore this kind of robe.
19 Tamar put ashes on her head and tore her robe. She put her hand on her head and walked away, crying bitterly.
20 Her brother Absalom said to her, "Has Amnon been with you? Be quiet for now, my sister. He is your brother. Do not take this to heart." So Tamar lived as an alone and sad woman in Absalom's house.
21 When King David heard all this, he was very angry.
22 Absalom never spoke to Amnon, good or bad. He hated Amnon for shaming his sister Tamar.
23 Two years later, Absalom had men cutting wool from his sheep at Baal-hazor near Ephraim. He invited all the king's sons.
24 He went to the king and said, "Your servant has hired men to cut the wool. Will the king and his servants please come with me?"
25 "No, my son," the king replied. "We should not all go. We would be too much for you." Absalom urged him, but he was not willing to go. So he gave him his blessing.
26 "If not," said Absalom, "please let my brother Amnon go with us." "Why should he go with you?" the king asked.
27 But Absalom urged him. So the king sent Amnon and the rest of his sons.

28 Absalom had told his young men, "Watch Amnon. When he is happy from drinking wine, I will give the order. 28b When I tell you to strike Amnon down, kill him. Do not be afraid. I have commanded you. Be brave and strong!"
29 So Absalom's young men did to Amnon just as Absalom had ordered. Then all the king's other sons got up and fled, each on his mule.
30 While they were on the way, a report reached David: "Absalom has struck down all the king's sons. Not one of them is left!"
31 Then the king stood up, tore his clothes, and lay on the ground. All his servants stood by with their clothes torn too.

32 But Jonadab, the son of David's brother Shimeah, spoke up. "My lord, they have not killed all the king's sons. Only Amnon is dead. 32b Absalom has planned this since the day Amnon forced his sister Tamar."
33 "So now, my lord the king, do not take this report to heart. Not all the king's sons are dead. Only Amnon is dead."

34 Meanwhile, Absalom had fled. The young man on watch looked up and saw many people coming down the road. They were on the side of the hill, west of him. 34b The watchman went and told the king, "I see men coming from Horonaim, along the side of the hill."
35 So Jonadab said to the king, "Look, the king's sons have arrived! It is just as your servant said."
36 As he finished speaking, the king's sons came in, crying loudly. Then the king and all his servants also wept very bitterly.
37 Absalom fled to Talmai son of Ammihud, the king of Geshur. But David mourned for his son every day.
38 After Absalom fled to Geshur, he stayed there three years.
39 King David longed to go to Absalom. He had been comforted about Amnon's death.

title: 2 Samuel 14
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 2 Samuel
paragraphs: 1 Now Joab son of Zeruiah saw that the king missed Absalom.

2 So Joab sent to Tekoa to bring a wise woman from there. He told her, "Please act like a sad woman. Put on clothes for sadness. Do not put oil on yourself. Act like a woman who has been sad for the dead a long time.
3 Then go to the king and say these words to him." And Joab put the words in her mouth.
4 When the woman from Tekoa went to the king, she bowed down to the ground and said, "Help me, O king!"
5 "What is wrong?" the king asked her. "Truly," she said, "I am a widow. My husband is dead.
6 Your servant had two sons. They were fighting in the field. No one was there to stop them. One son hit the other and killed him.

7 Now the whole family has risen up against your servant. They say, 'Hand over the one who struck down his brother. We will put him to death for the life of his brother. Then we will cut off the one who keeps the family name too!' So they would put out my last small fire. They would not keep my husband's name or future children on the earth."
8 "Go home," the king said to the woman, "and I will give orders for you."
9 But the woman of Tekoa said to the king, "My lord the king, let any blame be on me and on my father's house. May the king and his throne have no guilt."
10 "If anyone speaks to you," said the king, "bring him to me. He will not trouble you again!"

11 "Please," she replied, "may the king call on the LORD your God. Stop the man who wants to kill for blood from doing more harm. Then my son will not be destroyed!" "The LORD lives, and I promise," he said, "not even one hair of your son will fall to the ground."
12 Then the woman said, "Please, may your servant speak a word to my lord the king?" "Speak," he replied.

13 The woman asked, "Why have you planned a thing like this against the people of God? When the king says this, does he not judge himself as guilty? He has not brought back his own son who was sent away.
14 Surely we will die. We will be like water poured on the ground, which no one can gather up. Yet God does not take away a life. He plans ways so that the one sent away may not stay away from Him.
15 Now I have come to speak this matter to my lord the king because the people made me afraid. Your servant thought, 'I will speak to the king. Perhaps he will grant the request of his servant.
16 The king will hear and save his servant from the hand of the man who would cut off both me and my son from God's people.'
17 And now your servant says, 'May the word of my lord the king bring me rest. My lord the king can tell good from evil, just like the angel of God. May the LORD your God be with you.'"
18 Then the king said to the woman, "I am going to ask you something. Do not hide it from me!" "Let my lord the king speak," she replied.

19 So the king asked, "Is the hand of Joab behind all this?" The woman answered, "As surely as you live, my lord the king, no one can turn aside from what you say. Yes, your servant Joab is the one who gave me orders. He told your servant exactly what to say.
20 Joab your servant did this to bring about this new situation. But my lord has wisdom like the wisdom of the angel of God. You know everything that happens in the land."
21 Then the king said to Joab, "I now say yes to this. Go, bring back the young man Absalom."
22 Joab bowed down to the ground and blessed the king. "Today," said Joab, "your servant knows that he has pleased you, my lord the king. The king has said yes to his request."
23 So Joab got up, went to Geshur, and brought Absalom to Jerusalem.
24 But the king added, "He may return to his house, but he must not see my face." So Absalom returned to his own house. He did not see the king.
25 Now there was not a man in all Israel as good-looking and loved by all as Absalom. From his feet to his head, he had no fault at all.
26 He cut the hair of his head every year because his hair got so heavy. He would weigh it. It was about two hundred shekels by the king's weight.
27 Three sons were born to Absalom. He also had a daughter named Tamar, who was a beautiful woman.
28 Now Absalom lived in Jerusalem two years and did not see the face of the king.
29 Then he sent for Joab to send him to the king. But Joab refused to come to him. So Absalom sent a second time. But Joab still would not come.
30 Then Absalom said to his servants, "Look, Joab's field is next to mine. He has barley there. Go and set it on fire!" And Absalom's servants set the field on fire.
31 Then Joab came to Absalom's house and asked, "Why did your servants set my field on fire?"

32 "Look," said Absalom, "I sent for you and said, 'Come here. I want to send you to the king to ask: Why have I come back from Geshur? It would be better for me to be still there.' So now, let me see the king's face. If there is sin in me, let him kill me."
33 So Joab went and told the king. David called for Absalom, who came to him and bowed down before him. Then the king kissed Absalom.

title: 2 Samuel 15
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 2 Samuel
paragraphs: 1 Some time later, Absalom got a chariot with horses. Fifty men ran ahead of him.

2 He got up early and stood by the road to the city gate. When anyone had a problem to bring to the king, Absalom would call out. He asked, "What city are you from?" The man would reply, "Your servant is from one of the tribes of Israel."
3 Then Absalom would say, "Look, your case is good and right. But the king has no officer to listen to you."
4 He would also say, "If only someone made me judge in this land! Then everyone with a problem could come to me. I would give him justice."
5 When anyone came to bow down to him, Absalom would reach out his hand. He took hold of the man and kissed him.
6 Absalom did this to every Israelite who came to the king for justice. In this way, he won the hearts of the men of Israel.
7 After four years, Absalom said to the king, "Please let me go to Hebron. I want to keep a promise I made to the LORD.
8 Your servant made this promise while living in Geshur of Aram. I said, 'If the LORD brings me back to Jerusalem, I will worship the LORD in Hebron.'"
9 "Go in peace," said the king. So Absalom got up and went to Hebron.
10 Then Absalom sent spies through all the tribes of Israel. The message said, "When you hear the sound of the horn, shout, 'Absalom is king in Hebron!'"
11 Two hundred men from Jerusalem went with Absalom. They came as invited guests. They went with him in a simple way, for they knew nothing about the plan.

12 While Absalom was offering the sacrifices, he sent for Ahithophel the Gilonite. Ahithophel was David's adviser, and he came from his hometown of Giloh. So the secret plan grew strong, and more people kept joining Absalom.
13 Then a messenger came to David. He reported, "The hearts of the men of Israel are with Absalom."

14 David said to all his servants in Jerusalem, "Get up and let us run away! If we stay, we will not escape from Absalom. We must leave quickly. He will soon catch us, bring trouble on us, and kill the people in the city."
15 The king's servants replied, "Whatever our lord the king decides, we are your servants."
16 Then the king set out, and his whole household followed him. But he left behind ten second wives to take care of the palace.
17 So the king went out with all the people behind him. He stopped at the last house.
18 All his servants marched past him. The Cherethites and Pelethites went by. Six hundred Gittites who had followed him from Gath also went by.
19 Then the king said to Ittai the Gittite, "Why should you also come with us? Go back and stay with the new king. You are a foreigner who left his own land.

20 In fact, you arrived only yesterday. Should I make you wander with us today? I do not even know where I am going. Go back, and take your brothers with you. May the LORD show you steady love and faithfulness."

21 But Ittai answered the king, "As sure as the LORD lives, and as my lord the king lives, I will stay with you. Wherever my lord the king goes, your servant will be there. It does not matter if it means life or death."
22 "March on, then," said David to Ittai. So Ittai the Gittite marched past with all his men. The little children with him also went by.
23 Everyone in the countryside was weeping loudly as the people passed by. The king crossed the Kidron Valley, and all the people went toward the desert.
24 Zadok was also there. All the Levites with him were carrying the ark of God's covenant. They set down the ark of God. Abiathar offered sacrifices until all the people had left the city.
25 Then the king said to Zadok, "Take the ark of God back to the city. If I please the LORD, he will bring me back. He will let me see the ark and his home again.
26 But if he says, 'I am not pleased with you,' then here I am. Let him do to me what seems good to him."
27 The king also said to Zadok the priest, "Are you not a seer? Return to the city in peace. Take your son Ahimaaz with you, and take Abiathar with his son Jonathan.
28 Look, I will wait at the river crossings in the desert. I will stay there until word comes from you."
29 So Zadok and Abiathar took the ark of God back to Jerusalem, and they stayed there.

30 But David kept going up the Mount of Olives. He was weeping as he went. His head was covered, and he walked barefoot. All the people with him also covered their heads. They went up and wept as they walked.
31 Then someone told David, "Ahithophel is with the people in the secret plan with Absalom." So David prayed, "O LORD, please make Ahithophel's advice foolish!"
32 David came to the top of the hill, where he used to worship God. Hushai the Archite was there to meet him. His coat was torn, and dust was on his head.
33 David said to him, "If you go on with me, you will be a heavy load to me.
34 But you can block Ahithophel's advice for me. Return to the city and say to Absalom, 'I will be your servant, my king. In the past I was your father's servant, but now I will be your servant.'
35 Will not Zadok and Abiathar the priests be there with you? Tell them everything you hear from the king's palace.
36 Their two sons are also there with them — Ahimaaz son of Zadok and Jonathan son of Abiathar. Send them to me with everything you hear."
37 So David's friend Hushai arrived in Jerusalem just as Absalom was entering the city.

title: 2 Samuel 16
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 2 Samuel
paragraphs: 1 David walked a little past the top of the hill. Ziba, the servant of Mephibosheth, met him there. He brought two donkeys ready to ride. They carried 200 loaves of bread, 100 bunches of raisins, 100 pieces of summer fruit, and a bag of wine.

2 "Why do you have these?" the king asked. Ziba said, "The donkeys are for the king's family to ride. The bread and fruit are for the young men to eat. The wine will help tired people feel strong again in the wilderness."
3 "Where is your master's grandson?" the king asked. Ziba answered, "He is staying in Jerusalem. He said, 'Today, the people of Israel will give me my grandfather's kingdom.'"
4 So the king said to Ziba, "Now everything that belongs to Mephibosheth is yours!" Ziba said, "I bow low before you. May I please you, my lord the king!"
5 King David came near Bahurim. A man from Saul's family came out to meet him. His name was Shimei son of Gera. As he came near, he kept shouting curses.
6 He threw stones at David and at all the king's servants. The soldiers and strong men walked on David's right and left side.
7 As Shimei shouted curses, he said, "Get out, get out, you evil killer!"

8 "The LORD has punished you for all the blood of Saul's family. You took Saul's place as king. The LORD has given the kingdom to your son Absalom. Look, you have come to ruin because you are an evil killer!"
9 Then Abishai son of Zeruiah said to the king, "Why should this dead dog curse my lord the king? Let me go and cut off his head!"
10 But the king replied, "What have I to do with you, sons of Zeruiah? If he curses me because the LORD told him to, who can ask why?"

11 Then David said to Abishai and to all his servants, "Look, my own son wants to kill me. How much more, then, this man from Benjamin's tribe! Leave him alone and let him curse me, because the LORD told him to."
12 "Perhaps the LORD will see my trouble. He may give me good for the curses today."
13 So David and his men kept walking along the road. Shimei walked along the side of the hill across from him. As Shimei went, he shouted curses, threw stones, and threw dust at David.
14 At last, the king and all his people arrived, very tired. And there he rested.
15 Then Absalom and all the men of Israel came to Jerusalem. Ahithophel was with him.
16 David's friend Hushai the Archite went to Absalom. He said to him, "Long live the king! Long live the king!"
17 "Is this the love you show your friend?" Absalom replied. "Why did you not go with your friend?"
18 "Not at all," Hushai answered. "The LORD, the people, and all the men of Israel have chosen one man. I will belong to him, and I will stay with him."
19 "Also, whom should I serve if not the son? I served in your father's presence. So I will also serve in yours."
20 Then Absalom said to Ahithophel, "Give me advice. What should we do?"
21 Ahithophel replied, "Sleep with your father's concubines. He left them to take care of the palace. When all Israel hears that you made your father hate you, your followers will grow strong."
22 So they put up a tent for Absalom on the roof. He slept with his father's concubines in front of all Israel.
23 In those days, the advice of Ahithophel was like hearing God's own word. Both David and Absalom valued Ahithophel's advice this much.

title: 2 Samuel 17
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 2 Samuel
paragraphs: 1 Then Ahithophel said to Absalom, "Let me choose twelve thousand men. I will go after David tonight.
2 I will attack him while he is tired and weak. I will make him afraid, and all his people will run away. I will kill only the king.
3 Then I will bring all the people back to you, like a bride going home to her husband. You only want one man dead. Then all the people will be safe."
4 This plan seemed good to Absalom and all the leaders of Israel.
5 Then Absalom said, "Call Hushai the Archite too. Let us hear what he says."
6 So Hushai came to Absalom. Absalom said, "Ahithophel has said this plan. Should we do it? If not, what do you say?"
7 Hushai replied, "This time Ahithophel's advice is not good."

8 He went on, "You know your father and his men. They are strong fighters. They are as fierce as a wild bear that has lost her babies. Your father is a soldier. He will not stay with his soldiers at night.
9 By now he is hiding in a cave or some other place. If some of your soldiers die first, people will hear and say, 'Absalom's men have lost a battle.'
10 Then even the bravest soldier with a lion's heart will feel weak from fear. All Israel knows your father is a strong man with brave men.
11 Instead, I say this: gather all Israel to you, from Dan to Beersheba. They will be as many as the sand on the beach. You yourself should lead them to the fight.
12 Then we will attack David wherever we find him. We will fall on him like morning dew on the ground. Not even one of his men will be left.
13 If he runs to a city, all Israel will bring ropes there. We will pull that city down into the valley. Not even a small stone will be left."

14 Then Absalom and all the men of Israel said, "Hushai the Archite's advice is better than Ahithophel's." For the LORD had decided to stop Ahithophel's good advice. The LORD wanted to bring trouble on Absalom.
15 So Hushai told Zadok and Abiathar the priests, "Ahithophel told Absalom and the leaders of Israel one plan. I told them another plan.
16 Now send word quickly and tell David. Say, 'Do not stay tonight at the river crossings in the desert. Cross over right away. If not, the king and all his people will be destroyed.'"
17 Now Jonathan and Ahimaaz were staying at En-rogel. A servant girl would come and bring news to them. Then they would go and tell King David. They could not be seen entering the city.
18 But a young man saw them and told Absalom. So the two men left quickly. They came to a man's house in Bahurim. He had a well in his yard, and they climbed down into it.
19 Then the man's wife took a cover and spread it over the mouth of the well. She spread grain on top so no one would know.

20 Absalom's servants came to the woman at the house. They asked, "Where are Ahimaaz and Jonathan?" She said, "They have crossed the small river." The men searched but did not find them. So they went back to Jerusalem.
21 After the men left, Ahimaaz and Jonathan climbed out of the well. They went to tell King David. They said, "Get up and cross the river at once. Ahithophel has given this advice against you."
22 So David and all his people got up and crossed the Jordan. By morning, every person had crossed the Jordan.

23 Ahithophel saw that no one followed his advice. He put a saddle on his donkey and went home to his hometown. He settled all his things. Then he killed himself by hanging. So he died and was buried in his father's tomb.
24 Then David went to Mahanaim. Absalom crossed the Jordan with all the men of Israel.
25 Absalom had put Amasa over the army in place of Joab. Amasa was the son of Ithra the Ishmaelite. Ithra had married Abigail, the daughter of Nahash and sister of Zeruiah, the mother of Joab.
26 So the Israelites and Absalom camped in the land of Gilead.
27 When David came to Mahanaim, three men met him. They were Shobi son of Nahash from Rabbah of the Ammonites, Machir son of Ammiel from Lo-debar, and Barzillai the Gileadite from Rogelim.
28 They brought beds, bowls, and clay pots. They also brought wheat, barley, flour, roasted grain, beans, and lentils.
29 They brought honey, thick milk, sheep, and cheese from the cattle. The food was for David and his people. They said, "The people are hungry, very tired, and thirsty in the desert."

title: 2 Samuel 18
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 2 Samuel
paragraphs: 1 Then David counted his men. He chose leaders over hundreds and thousands.

2 He sent the army out in three parts. One third went under Joab. One third went under Joab's brother Abishai son of Zeruiah. One third went under Ittai the Gittite. The king said, "I will also march out with you."

3 But the people begged him, "You must not go out! If we run away, no one will care about us. Even if half of us die, they will not care. But you are worth ten thousand of us. It is better if you help us from the city."
4 "I will do what you think is best," the king replied. So he stood by the gate. All the troops marched out by hundreds and thousands.
5 The king had ordered Joab, Abishai, and Ittai, "Be gentle with young Absalom for me." All the people heard the king's orders about Absalom.
6 So David's army marched out to fight Israel. The battle took place in the forest of Ephraim.
7 There David's men defeated the army of Israel. The killing was great that day — twenty thousand men died.
8 The battle spread over the whole land. That day the forest killed more men than the sword.

9 Absalom was riding on his mule. He met David's servants. The mule went under the thick branches of a large oak tree. Absalom's head got caught in the tree. The mule kept going. So he was left hanging in the air.
10 One of the men saw this. He told Joab, "I just saw Absalom hanging in an oak tree!"
11 "You saw him!" Joab cried. "Why did you not strike him down right there? I would have given you ten silver coins and a soldier's belt!"
12 The man replied, "Even for a thousand silver coins, I would not lift my hand against the king's son. We heard the king order you and Abishai and Ittai, 'Protect young Absalom for me.'
13 If I had risked my own life — nothing is hidden from the king — you would have left me alone."
14 But Joab said, "I will not waste time with you!" He took three spears in his hand. He thrust them through Absalom's heart while he was still alive in the oak tree.
15 Then ten young men who carried Joab's armor surrounded Absalom. They struck him and killed him.
16 Then Joab blew the trumpet. The troops stopped chasing Israel because Joab held them back.
17 They took Absalom and threw him into a large pit in the forest. They piled a huge mound of stones over him. Meanwhile, all the Israelites fled, each one to his home.

18 During his life, Absalom had built a tall stone for himself in the King's Valley. He had said, "I have no son to keep my name alive." So he gave the stone his own name. To this day it is called Absalom's Monument.
19 Then Ahimaaz son of Zadok said, "Please let me run and tell the king the good news. The LORD has paid back his enemies."
20 But Joab replied, "You are not the man to bring good news today. You may do it another day. But not today, because the king's son is dead."
21 So Joab said to a Cushite, "Go, tell the king what you have seen." The Cushite bowed to Joab and started running.
22 But Ahimaaz son of Zadok kept asking. He said to Joab, "Whatever happens, please let me also run behind the Cushite!" "My son," Joab replied, "why do you want to run? You will get no reward."
23 "No matter what, I want to run!" he replied. "Then run!" Joab told him. So Ahimaaz ran by way of the plain and passed the Cushite.
24 David was sitting between the two gates. The watchman went up to the roof of the gateway by the wall. He looked out and saw a man running alone.
25 He called out and told the king. "If he is alone," the king replied, "he brings good news." The first runner came closer.
26 Then the watchman saw another man running. He called out to the gatekeeper, "Look! Another man is running alone!" "This one also brings good news," said the king.
27 The watchman said, "The first man looks like Ahimaaz son of Zadok." "This is a good man," said the king. "He comes with good news."

28 Then Ahimaaz called out to the king, "All is well!" He bowed facedown before the king. He went on, "Blessed be the LORD your God! He has handed over the men who lifted their hands against my lord the king."
29 The king asked, "Is young Absalom all right?" Ahimaaz replied, "When Joab sent the king's servant and your servant, I saw great trouble. But I do not know what it was."
30 "Move aside," said the king, "and stand here." So he stepped aside.
31 Just then the Cushite arrived. He said, "May my lord the king hear good news. Today the LORD has paid back all who rose up against you!"

32 The king asked the Cushite, "Is young Absalom all right?" The Cushite replied, "May what happened to the young man happen to the enemies of my lord the king. May it happen to all who rise up to harm you."

33 The king trembled with sorrow. He went up to the room above the gate and wept. As he walked, he cried out, "O my son Absalom! My son, my son Absalom! If only I had died instead of you — O Absalom, my son, my son!"

title: 2 Samuel 19
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 2 Samuel
paragraphs: 1 Then someone told Joab, "The king is crying and grieving over Absalom."
2 That day's win became a sad day for all the people. They heard, "The king is very sad about his son."
3 So the people went back to the city quietly. They went like people who sneak away in shame after losing a battle.
4 But the king hid his face. He cried out loudly, "O my son Absalom! O Absalom, my son, my son!"
5 Then Joab went into the house. He said to the king, "Today you have shamed all your servants. They saved your life. They saved your sons and daughters, your wives, and your second wives.
6 You love those who hate you. You hate those who love you! Today you made it clear. The army leaders and soldiers mean nothing to you. If Absalom were alive and we were all dead, you would be glad."

7 "Now get up! Go out and speak kindly to your servants. I swear by the LORD: if you do not go out, no one will stay with you tonight. This will be worse than all the trouble you have known since your youth."

8 So the king got up and sat at the city gate. All the people heard, "Look, the king is sitting at the gate." So they all came before the king. Meanwhile, the Israelites had run away, each man to his home.
9 All the people in the tribes of Israel were arguing. They said, "The king saved us from our enemies. He saved us from the Philistines. But now he has left the land because of Absalom.
10 And Absalom, the man we chose as our king, has died in battle. So why do we say nothing about bringing the king back?"

11 Then King David sent this message to Zadok and Abiathar the priests. "Speak to the elders of Judah. Say, 'Why should you be the last to bring the king back to his palace? All Israel is already talking about it.
12 You are my brothers, my own family. So why should you be the last to bring the king back?'
13 And say to Amasa, 'Are you not my own family? May God punish me harshly if you do not become the leader of my army in place of Joab!'"
14 So he won the hearts of all the men of Judah as one man. They sent word to the king, "Return, you and all your servants."
15 So the king returned. When he arrived at the Jordan, the men of Judah came to Gilgal. They met him and helped him cross the Jordan.
16 Then Shimei son of Gera, a Benjamite from Bahurim, hurried down with the men of Judah. He went to meet King David.
17 A thousand men of Benjamin came with him. Ziba the servant in charge of Saul's house also came. He brought his fifteen sons and twenty servants. They rushed down to the Jordan before the king.
18 They crossed at the shallow place to help the king's family and to do what was good in his sight. When Shimei son of Gera crossed the Jordan, he fell down before the king.
19 He said, "My lord, do not punish me. Do not remember the wrong of your servant on the day my lord the king left Jerusalem. May the king not think about it.
20 Your servant knows that I have sinned. So here I am today. I am the first of all the house of Joseph to come down to meet my lord the king."
21 But Abishai son of Zeruiah said, "Should Shimei not be killed for this? He spoke against the LORD's king!"
22 David replied, "Sons of Zeruiah, what have I to do with you? Why should you be my enemies today? Should any man be killed in Israel today? Don't I know that today I am king over Israel?"
23 So the king said to Shimei, "You shall not die." And the king made a strong promise to him.

24 Then Mephibosheth, Saul's grandson, went down to meet the king. He had not cared for his feet or trimmed his beard or washed his clothes. He had not done this from the day the king left until he returned safely.
25 He came from Jerusalem to meet the king. The king asked him, "Mephibosheth, why did you not go with me?"
26 He replied, "My lord the king, I cannot walk well. I said, 'I will put a saddle on my donkey and ride with the king.' But my servant Ziba tricked me.
27 He told lies about your servant to my lord the king. Yet my lord the king is like the angel of God. So do what is good in your eyes.
28 All my grandfather's house deserves death from my lord the king. Yet you gave your servant a place at your table. What more right, then, do I have to keep asking the king?"
29 The king replied, "Why say any more? I say that you and Ziba will share the land."
30 And Mephibosheth said to the king, "Instead, let Ziba take it all! My lord the king has come safely to his own house."
31 Now Barzillai the Gileadite had come down from Rogelim. He came to cross the Jordan with the king and send him on his way.
32 Barzillai was very old, eighty years old. He was a very rich man. He had given food to the king while he stayed in Mahanaim.
33 The king said to Barzillai, "Cross over with me. I will give you food at my side in Jerusalem."
34 But Barzillai replied, "How many years of life do I have left? Should I go up to Jerusalem with the king?

35 I am now eighty years old. Can I tell the difference between good and bad? Can your servant taste what he eats or drinks? Can I still hear the voice of singing men and women? Why should your servant be more trouble to my lord the king?
36 Your servant could go only a short way past the Jordan with the king. Why should the king give me such a big gift?

37 Please let your servant return. I want to die in my own city near the grave of my father and mother. But here is your servant Chimham. Let him cross over with my lord the king. Do for him what is good in your sight."
38 The king replied, "Chimham will cross over with me. I will do for him what seems good to you. And I will do for you whatever you ask of me."
39 So all the people crossed the Jordan. Then the king crossed over. The king kissed Barzillai and blessed him. Then Barzillai returned home.
40 Then the king went on to Gilgal. Chimham crossed over with him. All the troops of Judah and half the troops of Israel helped the king along the way.

41 Soon all the men of Israel came to the king. They asked, "Why did our brothers, the men of Judah, take you away in secret? Why did they bring the king and his family across the Jordan with all David's men?"

42 All the men of Judah replied to the men of Israel, "We did this because the king is our family member. Why does this make you angry? Have we ever eaten at the king's expense? Have we taken anything for ourselves?"

43 The men of Israel answered, "We have ten parts in the king. So we have more right to David than you. Why then do you look down on us? Were we not the first to speak of bringing our king back?" But the men of Judah spoke even more strongly than the men of Israel.

title: 2 Samuel 20
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 2 Samuel
paragraphs: 1 A useless man named Sheba son of Bichri, a Benjamite, was there. He blew the trumpet and shouted, "We have no share in David. We have no share in Jesse's son. Every man, go home to your tent, Israel!"
2 So all the men of Israel left David and followed Sheba son of Bichri. But the men of Judah stayed with their king all the way from the Jordan to Jerusalem.

3 When David returned to his palace in Jerusalem, he gathered the ten wives he had left to care for the palace. He placed them in a house under guard. He gave them food, but he no longer slept with them. They were kept inside until they died, living without husbands.
4 Then the king said to Amasa, "Call the men of Judah to come to me within three days. And come back yourself."
5 So Amasa went to call Judah. But he took longer than the time the king had set.
6 And David said to Abishai, "Sheba son of Bichri will hurt us more than Absalom. Take my servants and chase him. He may find strong-walled cities and escape from us."
7 So Joab's men marched out of Jerusalem to chase Sheba son of Bichri. With them went the Cherethites, the Pelethites, and all the strong soldiers.
8 While they were at the great stone in Gibeon, Amasa joined them. Joab wore soldier's clothes, with a short sword on his belt. As he stepped forward, the short sword slipped out of its cover.
9 "Are you well, my brother?" Joab asked Amasa. With his right hand Joab took hold of Amasa's beard to kiss him.

10 Amasa did not see the short sword in Joab's hand. Joab stabbed him in the stomach, and his insides came out on the ground. Joab did not need to strike him again, for Amasa was dead. Then Joab and his brother Abishai chased Sheba son of Bichri.
11 One of Joab's young men stood near Amasa and said, "Whoever supports Joab, and whoever is for David, follow Joab!"
12 But Amasa lay in his blood in the middle of the road. The young man saw that all the troops stopped there. So he dragged the body off the road into a field. He threw a cloth over it.
13 As soon as Amasa's body was off the road, all the men went on with Joab to chase Sheba son of Bichri.
14 Sheba passed through all the tribes of Israel to Abel-beth-maacah. He also passed through the whole region of the Berites, who gathered and followed him.
15 Joab's troops came and surrounded Sheba in Abel-beth-maacah. They built a dirt slope against the outer wall of the city. All the troops with Joab were beating the wall to knock it down.
16 Then a wise woman called out from the city, "Listen! Listen! Please tell Joab to come here. I want to speak with him."
17 When he came near her, the woman asked, "Are you Joab?" "I am," he replied. "Listen to the words of your servant," she said. "I am listening," he answered.
18 Then the woman said, "Long ago people used to say, 'Ask for advice at Abel.' That is how they settled their problems.
19 I am among the peaceful and loyal in Israel. But you are trying to destroy a city that is a mother in Israel. Why would you destroy what belongs to the LORD?"
20 "Never!" Joab said. "I would never destroy or wipe out this city!

21 That is not the case. But a man named Sheba son of Bichri, from the hills of Ephraim, has lifted his hand against the king, against David. Hand him over alone, and I will leave the city." "Look," the woman replied, "his head will be thrown to you over the wall."

22 Then the woman went to all the people with her wise advice. They cut off the head of Sheba son of Bichri and threw it to Joab. So he blew the trumpet, and his men went away from the city, each to his own home. And Joab returned to the king in Jerusalem.
23 Now Joab was over the whole army of Israel. Benaiah son of Jehoiada was over the Cherethites and Pelethites.
24 Adoram was in charge of the forced workers. Jehoshaphat son of Ahilud was the record keeper.
25 Sheva was the writer. Zadok and Abiathar were priests.
26 And Ira the Jairite was David's priest.

title: 2 Samuel 21
